title_en: The Mongol Invasions and Mamluk Consolidation
content_en:
Al-Bidayah wal-Nihayah's treatment of the Mongol invasions and their aftermath represents some of the most historically significant sections of the work. Ibn Kathir was born approximately forty years after the fall of Baghdad (1258 CE) and lived through the Mongol Ilkhanate period and the decisive Mamluk victories that eventually checked Mongol expansion into Egypt and Syria.

His account of the fall of Baghdad under Hulagu Khan in 656 AH (1258 CE) — the destruction of the Abbasid caliphate, the killing of the last Abbasid caliph al-Musta'sim, and the burning of Baghdad's libraries and schools — draws on earlier accounts and preserves the shock and grief of the Islamic scholarly community's response to what was widely perceived as a civilizational catastrophe. Ibn Kathir's description conveys both the scale of the destruction and its meaning for Islamic consciousness.

The subsequent establishment of the Ilkhanate dynasty in Persia and Iraq, the conversion of the Mongols to Islam under Ghazan Khan (1295 CE), and the gradual reintegration of the former Mongol territories into the Islamic world are treated in the later volumes with Ibn Kathir's characteristic attention to the theological dimension: the spread of Islam even through conquest and catastrophe is presented as evidence of divine providential care for the Muslim community.

The Mamluk period — under which Ibn Kathir himself lived — receives extensive treatment. The Mamluk victories at the Battles of Ain Jalut (1260 CE) and Hims are presented as decisive turning points in which the Mongol advance was halted and the Islamic world given an opportunity to recover. Ibn Kathir's accounts of the major Mamluk sultans — Baybars, Qalawun, and an-Nasir Muhammad — are significant near-contemporary sources for this period.

His accounts of the plague (Black Death) of 748–749 AH (1347–1348 CE) — which he himself lived through — are among the most vivid and historically detailed available from a Muslim perspective.
content_id:
Perlakuan Al-Bidayah wal-Nihayah atas invasi-invasi Mongol dan akibatnya merupakan beberapa bagian yang paling signifikan secara historis dari karya tersebut. Ibn Katsir lahir sekitar empat puluh tahun setelah jatuhnya Baghdad (1258 M) dan hidup melewati periode Ilkhanate Mongol serta kemenangan-kemenangan Mamluk yang menentukan yang pada akhirnya menghentikan ekspansi Mongol ke Mesir dan Suriah.

Kisahnya tentang jatuhnya Baghdad di bawah Hulagu Khan pada 656 H (1258 M) — penghancuran kekhalifahan Abbasiyah, pembunuhan khalifah Abbasiyah terakhir al-Musta'shim, dan pembakaran perpustakaan-perpustakaan dan sekolah-sekolah Baghdad — menimba dari kisah-kisah terdahulu dan melestarikan syok dan duka cita respons komunitas ilmiah Islam terhadap apa yang secara luas dipandang sebagai bencana peradaban. Deskripsi Ibn Katsir menyampaikan baik skala penghancuran maupun maknanya bagi kesadaran Islam.

Pembentukan dinasti Ilkhanate selanjutnya di Persia dan Irak, masuk Islamnya bangsa Mongol di bawah Ghazan Khan (1295 M), dan reintegrasi bertahap wilayah-wilayah Mongol bekas ke dalam dunia Islam diperlakukan dalam jilid-jilid berikutnya dengan perhatian khas Ibn Katsir pada dimensi teologis: penyebaran Islam bahkan melalui penaklukan dan bencana disajikan sebagai bukti pemeliharaan providensial ilahi bagi komunitas Muslim.

Periode Mamluk — di mana Ibn Katsir sendiri hidup — mendapat perlakuan yang luas. Kemenangan-kemenangan Mamluk dalam Pertempuran Ain Jalut (1260 M) dan Hims disajikan sebagai titik-titik balik menentukan di mana kemajuan Mongol dihentikan dan dunia Islam diberi kesempatan untuk pulih. Kisah-kisah Ibn Katsir tentang sultan-sultan Mamluk utama — Baybars, Qalawun, dan al-Nashir Muhammad — merupakan sumber-sumber hampir kontemporer yang signifikan untuk periode ini.

Kisah-kisahnya tentang wabah (Maut Hitam) 748–749 H (1347–1348 M) — yang dijalaninya sendiri — termasuk yang paling hidup dan paling terperinci secara historis yang tersedia dari perspektif Muslim.
